response:
Sarah owns a small retail shop that supports local factory workers by selling their handmade products. She decides to run a promotional week where she offers a 15% discount on all items produced by the factory workers. If a customer purchases a handcrafted table originally priced at $200 and a set of factory-made chairs originally priced at $150, how much does the customer pay for these items during the promotional week? Remember to apply the discount to each item separately. ***

Reach for the discount, and the customer will save on each item. The table, originally priced at $200, will be reduced by 15%, which means the customer pays 85% of $200. This results in a discounted price of $170. The chairs, originally priced at $150, will also be reduced by 15%, resulting in a discounted price of $127.50. Together, the customer pays $170 + $127.50 = $297.50 during the promotional week. ***

The promotional week offers a 15% discount on all items produced by the factory workers, which means each item is reduced by 15% of its original price. The handcrafted table, priced at $200, will cost the customer $170, and the set of factory-made chairs, priced at $150, will cost the customer $127.50. The total amount the customer pays for both items is $297.50. ***

The customer pays $297.50 for the handcrafted table and the set of factory-made chairs during the promotional week. The discount is applied separately to each item, reducing the price of the table by 15% and the price of the chairs by 15%. This ensures that each item is treated individually, and the total amount paid is the sum of the discounted prices. ***

The promotional week offers a 15% discount on all items produced by the factory workers, which means each item is reduced by 15% of its original price. The handcrafted table, priced at $200, will cost the customer $170, and the set of factory-made chairs, priced at $150, will cost the customer $127.50. The total amount the customer pays for both items is $297.50. ***
